coredata.dc:title: The thermodynamic assessment of the Ti–Si–N system and the interfacial reaction analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xiaoyan
Li, Changrong
Zhang, Weijing
coredata.dc:description: A consistent thermodynamic data set for the Ti–Si–N ternary system is obtained by means of calculation of phase diagrams (CALPHAD) technology. The sublattice model is used to describe the ternary solid solution phases: (Ti,Si)1(N,Va)0.5 for (αTi), (Ti,Si)1(N,Va)3 for (βTi), (Ti,Si)1(N,Va)1 for TiN and (Si,Ti%)2(Si%,Ti)3Ti3(N,Va)1 for Ti5Si3N x . The intermetallic compounds, Ti3Si, Ti5Si4, TiSi, TiSi2, Si3N4 and Ti2N are treated as stoichiometric compounds. The liquid solution phase and the diamond-Si phase are assumed to be substitutional solutions with Redlich–Kister formula for the expression of the excess Gibbs energy. The gas phase is treated as an ideal mixture. Based on the present optimized data, the interfacial reactions of Ti/Si3N4 and TiN/Si couples are analysed and the phase formation sequences are predicted. The calculation results agree well with experiments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti–Si–N ternary
Phase diagram
Assessment
Phase formation sequence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Thermodynamic assessment of the Ti–Si–N system
Prediction of the phase formation sequence in the Ti/Si3N4 and TiN/Si diffusion couples
Phase formation sequence in Ti/Si3N joint
Phase formation sequence in TiN/Si joint

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated Ti–Si phase diagram according to the modeling by Seifert [9].
Calculated Si–N phase diagram according to the modeling by Ma et al. [10].
Calculated Ti–N phase diagram according to the modeling by Jonsson [11].
Ti–Si–N phase diagram from 973 to 1273K drawn by Beyers and Sinclair [21].
Ti–Si–N phase diagram at 1300K drawn by Wakelkamp [22].
Ti–Si–N phase diagram at 1273K drawn by Sambasivan and Petuskey [23].
Ti–Si–N phase diagram at 1373K drawn by Paulasto et al. [7].
Ti–Si–N phase diagram at 1273K edited by Rogl and Shuster [24].
(a) Calculated Ti–Si–N isopleth at 0.1at.% N. (b) Enlarged central part of (a) (T 1 is Ti5Si3N x ).
The Ti–Si–N isothermal section at 1373K and phase sequence in Ti/Si3N4 couple.
The Ti–Si–N isothermal section at 1373K and phase sequence in TiN/Si couple.
The information of phase equilibria at Ti–Si–N ternary system a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: “√” denotes the existed tie lines and “*” denotes that the contradictory experimental results were obtained on the existence of the tie line.

Binary and ternary parameters of Ti–Si–N phases parameter= a + bT + cT ln(T)+ dT 2 + e
T −1 + fT −3 + gT 3

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The parameters marked with an asterisk (*) were obtained in this paper. All parameters were given in SI units (J, mol, K). Parameters for pure solids and liquids were from Dinsdale [25].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated driving force at 1373K for Ti/Si3N4 interface at x Si =1×10−2 and x N =5×10−2
Calculated driving force at 1373K for Ti5Si3N x /Si3N4 interface at x Si =3.37×10−1 and x N =8×10−2

article.ce:title: The thermodynamic assessment of the Ti–Si–N system and the interfacial reaction analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology at Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A consistent thermodynamic data set for the Ti–Si–N ternary system is obtained by means of calculation of phase diagrams (CALPHAD) technology. The sublattice model is used to describe the ternary solid solution phases: (Ti,Si)1(N,Va)0.5 for (αTi), (Ti,Si)1(N,Va)3 for (βTi), (Ti,Si)1(N,Va)1 for TiN and (Si,Ti%)2(Si%,Ti)3Ti3(N,Va)1 for Ti5Si3N x . The intermetallic compounds, Ti3Si, Ti5Si4, TiSi, TiSi2, Si3N4 and Ti2N are treated as stoichiometric compounds. The liquid solution phase and the diamond-Si phase are assumed to be substitutional solutions with Redlich–Kister formula for the expression of the excess Gibbs energy. The gas phase is treated as an ideal mixture. Based on the present optimized data, the interfacial reactions of Ti/Si3N4 and TiN/Si couples are analysed and the phase formation sequences are predicted. The calculation results agree well with experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium nitride films have been widely applied to many fields, for example coatings on cutting tools, bearings, and forming tools since their excellent properties such as high hardness and low coefficient of friction. However, titanium nitride alone cannot be used at the relatively high temperature in air due to its poor chemical stability [1]. In order to improve the chemical and mechanical properties of the film, intensive efforts have been made to explore multi-component materials such as Ti–Al–N and Ti–Si–N [2,3]. Ti–Si–N system is attracting much interest for its much higher hardness value and better stability at elevated temperature than the TiN coating [4–6].

Paulasto et al. [7] investigated the stability and formation kinetics of TiN and silicides in Ti/Si3N4 and TiN/Si diffusion joints and studied experimentally the interfacial reactions in these diffusion couples in the temperature range between 1223 and 1373K. Gotman and Gutmanas [8] observed the sequences of the phase formation at the Si3N4/Ti powder interface at different temperatures (1173, 1223 and 1323K).

For the better understanding of the Ti–Si–N system, it is necessary to describe its thermodynamic properties after the critical optimization. Based on the present assessment, the interfacial reactions and the phase formation sequences of the Ti/Si3N4 and TiN/Si diffusion couples are analysed and predicted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic assessment of the Ti–Si–N system

ce:section.ce:para: The parameters of the Ti–Si, Si–N and Ti–N systems were taken from Seifert [9], Ma et al. [10] and Jonsson [11], respectively.

Ti–N system has been assessed by many authors [11–13]. For a consistent dataset, the data of Ti–N system were taken from Jonsson [11] who successfully calculated the Ti–C–N [14], Ti–W–N [15] and Ti–W–C [16] systems.

The calculated Ti–Si, Ti–N and Si–N binaries are shown in Figs. 1–3
.

Many investigators have studied the phase equilibria and the isothermal sections of the Ti–Si–N ternary system. Their discrepancy mainly focused on the existence of four tie lines between TiN–TiSi, TiSi2–Si3N4, TiN–Si and Ti3Si–(αTi), respectively.

The results of all investigations are illustrated in Table 1 and Figs. 4–8
.

Ting [17] found that no reaction occurred between a TiN layer and a silicon substrate after 30-min heat treatment at 1173K, confirming the existence of the TiN–Si tie line. Borisov et al. [18] formed the mixtures of TiN and TiSi2, TiN and TiSi, and TiN and Ti5Si3N x and proved the existence of TiN–TiSi2, TiN–TiSi, and TiN–Ti5Si3N x tie lines. Desu and Taylor [19] detected three tie lines: TiN–TiSi2, TiN–Ti5Si3N x and TiN–Si3N4. Based on the experimental data and the Gibbs free energy data from Barin and Knacke [20], Beyers and Sinclair [21] drew the isothermal section of Ti–Si–N ternary system in the temperature range 973–1273K (see Fig. 4).

Wakelkamp [22] revised the ternary phase diagram of Beyers and Sinclair [21] at 1300K, extending the compositional ranges of TiN and Ti5Si3N x , adding the compounds Ti3Si, Ti5Si4 and the terminal solution phase (αTi), and pointing out the existence of TiN–TiSi2 tie line (see Fig. 5). As for the existence of the tie line TiN–TiSi, contradictory experimental results were reported. On the one hand, the reaction between TiN and TiSi at 1373K caused the formation of a Ti5Si3N x layer with precipitates of TiSi2. On the other hand, the reaction between hot pressed silicon nitride and TiSi suggested the direct contact between TiN and TiSi.

On the basis of the work of Beyers and Sinclair [21] and Wakelkamp [22], Sambasivan and Petuskey [23] again revised the Ti–Si–N ternary phase diagram at 1273K and gave the compositional boundaries for Ti5Si3N x schematically (see Fig. 6). The existence of TiN–TiSi tie line was reported.

The phase diagrams provided by Refs. [21–23] show the equilibrium between TiN and Si but not between TiSi2 and Si3N4. But Paulasto et al. [7] pointed out that TiN and Si could react with each other and form Si3N4 and/or TiSi2 depending on the activity of nitrogen (see Fig. 7). Rogl and Shuster [24] also showed the existence of the tie line TiSi2–Si3N4 at 1273K (see Fig. 8). Furthermore the equilibrium between Ti3Si and (αTi) was proved by Paulasto et al. [7].

From the results of Refs. [7,19,21–23], it can be proposed that there might be no conflict as to whether the tie line between TiN–TiSi exists or not. Below and above 1300K may correspond to the existence and the non-existence of this tie line, respectively. Much attention will be paid to this possibility in the present assessment.

The L parameters are interaction parameters between the species given in subscript. Eq. (6) is often referred to as the Redlich–Kister equation [28]. The binary interaction parameters were taken from Refs. [9–11]. The ternary ones were optimized in the present work.

The substitutional solution model (Si, Ti), was used for the diamond-Si phase:

There are three titanium ternary solution phases: (βTi) phase, (αTi) phase and TiN which were described with the two-sublattice model developed by Hillert and Staffansson [29] and expressed by the general formula (Si,Ti)1(N,Va) a . Va denotes vacancy. The subscript a is the number of interstitial sites per Ti atom, which is equal to 3 for (βTi), 0.5 for (αTi) and 1 for TiN.

If φ denotes one of the phases (βTi), (αTi) and TiN, the Gibbs energy per mole of formula unit is given by
(8)

the Gibbs energies of the hypothetical nitrides also in φ modification.

The ternary phase Ti5Si3N x is the ternary extension of the binary phase Ti5Si3. Ti5Si3 was modeled with the sublattice model (Si,Ti%)2(Si%,Ti)3Ti3 [9], where % represented the major component in the related sublattice. Ti5Si3 crystallizes in the Mn5Si3-type (D88) with space group P63/mcm [30] where the interstitials of the crystal structure could be completely filled with small atoms giving a continuous range of homogeneity as Ti5Si3Q (Q=B, C, N, O). Those small atoms Q occupied the fourth sublattice [31]. It was reported that the solubility of nitrogen in Ti5Si3 was about 11at.% N [22], the same value as carbon in Ti5Si3 [32]. So it can be concluded that Ti5Si3N x has similar properties with Ti5Si3C x to some extent. Since the sublattice model (Si,Ti%)2(Si%,Ti)3Ti3(C,Va)1 was used to describe the ternary solid solution phase Ti5Si3C x
[33] (Si,Ti%)2(Si%,Ti)3Ti3(N,Va)1 was taken for Ti5Si3N x in this paper and its Gibbs energy was given by the following expression:
(9)

There are six binary stoichiometric compounds, Ti3Si, Ti5Si4, TiSi, TiSi2, Si3N4 and Ti2N.

From Ref. [9], the Gibbs energy function for Ti q Si p (Ti3Si, Ti5Si4, TiSi and TiSi2) could be expressed by Eq. (10):
(10)

From Ref. [10], the Gibbs energy for the stoichiometric compound Si3N4 had a similar expression as for the pure elements, Eq. (1).

From Ref. [11], The Gibbs energy of Ti2N was given relative to

( T )

The optimization of thermodynamic parameters has been carried out with the help of the PARROT module of Thermo-Calc software developed by Sundman et al. [34]. The working strategy is the minimization of the square sum of the difference between experimental data and computed values. In order to obtain internally consistent data which comply with the rules of thermodynamics and reflect the phase equilibria of the system, the following steps are adopted: (i) selecting the appropriate model for each phase; (ii) simplifying the thermodynamic descriptions by reducing the number of parameters according to reasonable classification; (iii) using trial and error method to assess the system while considering all aspects of experimental data until most of them fall within the experimental uncertainties. For Ti5Si3N x phase, four hypothetical compounds Ti3Si5N, Ti6Si2N, Ti5Si3N and Ti8N were introduced as shown in Eq. (9). Among them, the Gibbs energy of Ti5Si3N,
G
T
i 2
: S
i 3
: T
i 3
: N
T
i 5
S
i 3
N x
0
, is the most sensitive parameter for the contour of Ti5Si3N x phase.

For simplicity, the ternary interaction parameters for liquid
L
N , Si , Ti
L
were treated as zero in the optimization.

Since the silicon solubility in titanium solution (βTi), (αTi) and TiN phase, was very low, the Gibbs energy of the hypothetical compound SiN a ,
G
Si :
N a
φ
0
( T )
, and the interaction parameters
L
Si , Ti : N
φ
0
and
L
Si : N , Va
φ
0
were set to zero in the assessment.

Meanwhile, the ternary interaction parameters
L
Si , Ti : Si : Ti : N
T
i 5
S
i 3
N x
0
and
L
Si , Ti : Ti : Ti : N
T
i 5
S
i 3
N x
0
had little influence on the phase relation of Ti5Si3N x solution and they were set to zero.

According to the thermodynamic data provided by Seifert [9] for Ti–Si binary, Ma et al. [10] for Si–N binary, Jonsson [11] for Ti–N binary, and Dinsdale [25] for unary, the assessed Ti–Si–N ternary phase diagram showed the equilibrium between TiSi2 and Si3N4, but not TiN–Si. This result is consistent with Refs. [7,24].

Fig. 9 is the calculated isopleth at 0.1at.% N. It is shown in Figs. 2 and 9 that the homogeneity region of Ti5Si3 or Ti5Si3N x (T 1) becomes smaller and smaller with decreasing temperature. In the low temperature range as shown in Fig. 10 , TiN is in the equilibria with Ti5Si3N x , Ti5Si4, TiSi and TiSi2, respectively. With the increase of the temperature, the two-phase equilibria, TiN+Ti5Si4 and TiN+TiSi, disappear one after another as shown in Figs. 11 and 12
.
The tie line Ti3Si-(αTi) is shown in Figs. 10–13 , consistent with the results of Paulasto et al. [7].
The thermodynamic description used for the calculation is shown in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction of the phase formation sequence in the Ti/Si3N4 and TiN/Si diffusion couples
ce:section.ce:para:
For an internal process system, under the condition of constant T and P, the driving force for the formation of a small amount of a new phase, the driving force for the nucleation of a new phase, is the decrease of Gibbs energy accompanying the precipitation of one mole of θ phase from a large amount of supersaturated ϕ solution. By convention, the change of Gibbs energy for a reaction is defined as
Δ
G m
=
G m
product
−
G m
reactant
. In Thermo-Calc software, the decrease in Gibbs energy, −ΔG m, is defined as the driving force for the formation of a new phase:
D θ
= −
Δ
G m
=
G m
reactant
−
G m
product
. In quantity, the driving force for a phase is the shortest distance between the Gibbs energy surface of the phase and the plane made up by joining the chemical potentials for all components in the system at the equilibrium state.

The driving force for a stable phase is equal to zero, while positive and negative for a spontaneous and an impossible precipitation of the phase respectively. By considering the driving force for the formation of a new phase, it can be concluded whether the phase precipitates or not can be predicted.

In the Thermo-Calc software, there are three types of the defined status for a phase during the equilibrium calculation: ENTERED, SUSPEND and DORMANT. ENTERED or SUSPEND means that the phase is included or excluded in the calculation respectively, while DORMANT means that the phase is not considered in the calculation but its driving force for precipitation is calculated.

Based on our assessment for Ti–Si–N ternary system and the experimental data from literatures, using the Thermo-Calc software, the phase formation sequences are predicted between Ti/Si3N4 and between TiN/Si joints.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase formation sequence in Ti/Si3N joint
ce:section.ce:para: Refs. [7,8] studied the Ti/Si3N4 couple experimentally and similar results were obtained. The schematic diagram of the phase sequence in Ti/Si3N4 joint at 1373K was given by Paulasto et al. [7].

Since the diffusion of N atoms in (βTi) and (αTi) is fast, at the beginning of the reaction between Ti and Si3N4, the equilibrium of (βTi) with (αTi) can be reached rapidly. So for the Ti/Si3N4 joint, the initial status was set as ENTERED for (βTi), (αTi) and Si3N4 phases and DORMANT for other phases. The starting point of the calculation was supposed to be x Si =1×10−2, x N =5×10−2. Table 3 shows the calculated driving force at 1373K for the Ti/Si3N4 interface.

It is shown in Table 3 that the driving force of the Ti5Si3N x phase is positive and is the largest. So the Ti5Si3N x phase will precipitate at the interface between Ti and Si3N4 and two new interfaces, Ti/Ti5Si3N x and Ti5Si3N x /Si3N4, will be formed. At the mean time, the N atom diffuses towards Ti5Si3N x and changes its composition from x Si =3.62×10−1 and x N =5.78×10−4 to x Si =3.37×10−1 and x N =8×10−2. According to the equilibrium calculation the Ti5Si3N x phase was put into the equilibrium calculation and its status changed to ENTERED. Then the driving force at 1373K for the two interfaces was calculated. The calculated results for Ti5Si3N x /Si3N4 interface are shown in Table 4 . Those for Ti/Ti5Si3N x are not listed since no positive driving force was obtained.

It is shown in Table 4 that the driving force of the TiN phase is positive and maximum. So the TiN phase will be the second to precipitate at the interface between Ti5Si3N x and Si3N4. Again two new interfaces, Ti5Si3N x /TiN and TiN/Si3N4, will be formed. Put TiN phase into the equilibrium calculation and change its status to ENTERED. Then calculate the driving force for the two interfaces.
The calculation results for Ti5Si3N x /TiN and TiN/Si3N4 interface also did not reveal the new phase precipitation.
It is predicted from the calculation above that the phase sequence is Ti–(αTi)–Ti5Si3N x –TiN–Si3N4 in Ti/Si3N4 couple at 1373K (see Fig. 14 ). This is consistent with the experimental results [7,8].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase formation sequence in TiN/Si joint
ce:section.ce:para: For TiN/Si joint, the initial status was set as ENTERED for TiN, and Si phases and DORMANT for other phases at 1373K. The starting point of the calculation was x Si =1×10−4, x N =4.98×10−1. The calculation showed that the driving force of the TiSi2 phase is positive and maximum. So the TiSi2 phase will precipitate at the interface between TiN and Si. And two new interfaces TiN/TiSi2 and TiSi2/Si will be formed.

Since the Si reacts with TiN to form TiSi2, this would consume part of the titanium in TiN and enrich the N content at the interface. So for the TiN/TiSi2 interface, the calculation point was set to x Si =0, x N =0.5. The driving force of the Si3N4 phase was calculated to be positive and maximum at the interface TiN/TiSi2, where the Si3N4 phase will precipitate. Similar experimental results were reported by Paulasto et al. [7]. When the TiN/Si joint annealed in vacuum furnace at the temperature of 1373K, a pronounced TiSi2 layer would be formed next to TiN layer. Due to the TiSi2 layer formed above the TiN, nitrogen was not able to vaporize and this led to the formation of Si3N4 layer.
It is predicted from the calculation above that the phase sequence is TiN–Si3N4–TiSi2–Si in TiN/Si joint at 1373K (see Fig. 15 ). This is consistent with the experimental results [7].

In this paper, all the information from literature in the Ti–Si–N ternary has been critically evaluated. Using CALPHAD technology, consistent thermodynamic data are optimized rigorously, while considering the following three aspects. (1) Many investigators reported the tie lines TiSi2–Si3N4 and TiN–Si which cannot coexist at the same time. The present assessed results support the existence of TiSi2–Si3N4 tie lines. (2) The Ti5Si3 phase is extended to a ternary solution Ti5Si3N x which is described with the sublattice model (Si,Ti%)2(Si%,Ti)3Ti3(N,Va)1. At low temperatures, the composition range of Ti5Si3N x is narrow and it becomes larger and larger with increasing temperature. The change of the composition space leads to different equilibrium relationship at high and low temperatures. (3) TiN can form equilibria with Ti5Si3N x , Ti5Si4, TiSi, TiSi2 and Si3N4, etc., while the tie lines TiN–Ti5Si4 and TiN–TiSi disappear one after another at the relatively high temperature.

Based on the present assessment and the driving force analysis, the phase sequences are predicted, Ti–(αTi)–Ti5Si3N x –TiN–Si3N4 for a Ti/Si3N4 joint and TiN–Si3N4–TiSi2–Si for a TiN/Si joint at 1373K. The calculated results are compared with the experimental data. And satisfactory agreement is achieved.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to express their appreciation to the Royal Institute of Technology Sweden for supplying the Thermo-Calc software. This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 50071008) and the National Doctorate Fund of the State Education Ministry of China (No. 20030008016).

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: W. Wakelkamp, Ph.D. Thesis, University of Eindhoven, Eindhoven, The Netherlands, 1991.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phase Diagrams of Ternary Boron Nitride and Silicon Systems

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: SGTE Substance Database, Royal Institute of Technology, Sweden, 1994.

sb:publisher.sb:name: American Institute of Physics for the National Institute of Standards and Technology
